title: SEGMENT 7 — Revivicor: the company that made Bennett's heart

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — Revivicor: the company that made Bennett's heart
So let's talk about Revivicor.
If there is a hero company in this story — or at least a protagonist company — it is this one.
Revivicor's history is, in itself, a tour of the last quarter century of mammalian biotechnology.
The lineage begins, as I hinted earlier, with PPL Therapeutics, the Edinburgh-based biotech that, in 1996, cloned Dolly the sheep with the Roslin Institute.
After Dolly, PPL pivoted into making genetically engineered farm animals as biological factories — pigs that could produce human pharmaceuticals in their milk, that sort of thing.
In 2003, PPL split off its xenotransplantation program into a new company called Revivicor.
The name is a portmanteau, roughly "revive" plus a Latin root for "heart.
" It tells you what they cared about.
Revivicor's bet, from the start, was that genetically engineered pig organs were going to save human lives.
For the first decade, the bet was lonely.
The 2008 financial crisis nearly killed the company.
They were acquired in 2011 by a larger group called United Therapeutics, which is a publicly traded pharmaceutical company best known for cardiopulmonary drugs.

The deal was, in the parlance of biotech, a strategic acquisition — United Therapeutics' founder, Martine Rothblatt, had a personal stake in the field, because her own daughter has a serious lung disease, and Rothblatt had become convinced that xenotransplantation was the eventual answer to the donor lung shortage.
So Revivicor went from being a struggling spinout to being the well-funded subsidiary of a Fortune-list pharma, with a leader who genuinely believed in the mission.
The Revivicor pigs are, broadly, the lineal descendants of the 2002 GalT knockout work.
Over the 2010s, Revivicor stacked additional edits — initially through breeding and cloning, then increasingly through CRISPR — onto the original GalT-KO line.
By the late 2010s, they had pigs with the triple knockout, multiple human complement regulators, anticoagulant transgenes, and a growth hormone receptor knockout.
They called the final clinical product the 10-gene-edited pig.
The word "edited" is doing some work here — some of the 10 edits are knockouts, some are knockins, some are combinations.
But ten distinct genetic interventions.
Revivicor's pigs, importantly, do not have PERV knockouts.

The company's position is that with their gnotobiotic herd management — meaning the pigs are raised in barrier facilities, screened intensively for pathogens, and isolated from each other — the PERV risk is acceptable without inactivating the retroviruses in the genome.
It's a defensible position, and the FDA agreed for Bennett's surgery.
But it is a meaningful difference from the eGenesis approach.
Revivicor is based in Blacksburg, Virginia, and its pig facility — a high-containment, climate-controlled barrier facility — is in the Virginia hill country.
The pigs live in clean rooms.
Their feed is sterilized.
Their water is filtered.
The humans who work with them shower in and shower out, wear sterile garments, and are screened for human pathogens that might cross over to the pigs.
It is, again, more semiconductor fab than barn.
The Revivicor herd is, in a sense, the most expensively maintained set of pigs in human history.

In 2020, the FDA granted approval — under a peculiar regulatory pathway called the Intentional Genomic Alteration designation — for Revivicor's GalT-KO pigs to be used as food, marketed under the name GalSafe.
This was for a different application: people with alpha-gal syndrome, an allergy to mammalian meat caused by tick bites, can eat GalSafe pork without the allergic reaction.
It was the first FDA approval of any genetically engineered animal for both food and biomedical use.
It doesn't really affect the transplant program, but it was a regulatory milestone — proof that the agency would, in principle, approve products from a genetically engineered pig herd.
The 10-edit Revivicor heart is the heart that went into David Bennett in January 2022.
We'll get to him in two segments.
Before that, the other company.
